landmarkChecklist: Stay high on the right side of the trachea.
Keep the target above 4R and above the azygos handoff.
Use the left lateral tracheal border rule for laterality rather than the tracheal midline.
Think superior right paratracheal corridor, not hilar gateway.

ct.title: Axial CT Correlate
ct.focusLabel: Right of trachea, superior paratracheal stripe
ct.caption: Start with the superior-right stack relationship, then confirm the node remains right of the left lateral tracheal border.

bronchoscopy.title: Bronchoscopic Correlate
bronchoscopy.caption: This is still a tracheal view; if it feels hilar, you have likely drifted too far distal.

ultrasound.title: EBUS Correlate
ultrasound.caption: Stack it above 4R and verify laterality with the tracheal border rule before assigning stage.

quizItems.prompt: Which station is highlighted in this high right paratracheal CT view?
quizItems.explanation: The correct answer is 2R because the target remains high, right-sided, and superior to the 4R level.

landmarkChecklist: Stay high on the left side of the trachea.
Keep the target above the 4L boundary.
Use the left lateral tracheal border rule before calling laterality.
This is still a tracheal station, not a hilar one.

ct.title: Axial CT Correlate
ct.focusLabel: Left of trachea, superior paratracheal stripe
ct.caption: Read 2L as the superior left paratracheal target before the 4L and 10L transitions.

bronchoscopy.title: Bronchoscopic Correlate
bronchoscopy.caption: If the view feels distal or branch-level, you are likely no longer at 2L.

ultrasound.title: EBUS Correlate
ultrasound.caption: Confirm left-sided position and keep the target above the 4L handoff.

quizItems.prompt: Which station is highlighted from the superior left tracheal wall perspective?
quizItems.explanation: The correct answer is 2L because the target remains superior and left paratracheal rather than lower or hilar.

landmarkChecklist: Place 4R below 2R and above 10R.
Use the inferior azygos margin as the lower boundary.
Keep the target paratracheal rather than hilar.
Remember that the left lateral tracheal border still divides right from left.

ct.title: Axial CT Correlate
ct.caption: Anchor 4R beneath 2R and above the azygos-defined hilar handoff.

bronchoscopy.title: Bronchoscopic Correlate
bronchoscopy.orientation: Right lateral tracheal wall near the carinal descent.
bronchoscopy.caption: This is still tracheal and paratracheal; a main-bronchus feel suggests 10R instead.

ultrasound.title: EBUS Correlate
ultrasound.caption: Check the azygos boundary before calling the node hilar instead of paratracheal.

quizItems.prompt: Which station is highlighted in this right lower paratracheal EBUS view?
quizItems.explanation: The correct answer is 4R because the target is still lower paratracheal and has not passed below the inferior azygos margin into 10R.

landmarkChecklist: Place 4L below 2L and above 10L.
Use the upper rim of the left main pulmonary artery as the lower boundary.
Keep 4L medial to the ligamentum arteriosum.
Do not confuse a paratracheal target with an AP-window or hilar target.

ct.title: Axial CT Correlate
ct.caption: Use the pulmonary artery boundary and the medial ligamentum relationship to separate 4L from neighboring stations.

bronchoscopy.title: Bronchoscopic Correlate
bronchoscopy.orientation: Left paratracheal survey near the carina.
bronchoscopy.caption: The view should still feel mediastinal rather than left hilar.

ultrasound.title: EBUS Correlate
ultrasound.caption: The biggest confusion pair is 10L, so confirm that the node remains above the superior left main pulmonary artery margin.

quizItems.prompt: Which station is highlighted in this lower left paratracheal CT panel?
quizItems.explanation: The correct answer is 4L because the node remains left paratracheal and above the 10L boundary.

landmarkChecklist: Find the carina first.
Keep the target between the medial margins of the main bronchi.
Remember that station 7 is midline rather than right- or left-lateralized.
Use this station as an orientation reset point.

ct.title: Axial CT Correlate
ct.caption: If you lose orientation, station 7 is the quickest midline reset and staging anchor.

bronchoscopy.title: Bronchoscopic Correlate
bronchoscopy.orientation: Carinal bifurcation with a target just beneath it.
bronchoscopy.caption: Stay centered below the bifurcation; drifting laterally often means you are moving into station 10 territory.

ultrasound.title: EBUS Correlate
ultrasound.caption: Think between the medial bronchial margins rather than simply 'somewhere central.'

quizItems.prompt: Which station is highlighted directly beneath the carinal bifurcation?
quizItems.explanation: The correct answer is station 7 because the target is centered beneath the carina and between the medial main bronchial margins.

landmarkChecklist: Place 10R below 4R and before 11R branch-level targets.
Use the inferior azygos margin as the upper boundary.
Check whether the node is outside the medial main bronchial margins rather than in station 7.
This is a main-bronchus hilar cue, not yet a branch-level cue.

ct.title: Axial CT Correlate
ct.caption: The key distinction is hilar handoff rather than interlobar branching.

bronchoscopy.title: Bronchoscopic Correlate
bronchoscopy.orientation: Right main bronchus before deeper branch points.
bronchoscopy.caption: Stay at the main-bronchus hilar gateway before moving into 11Rs or 11Ri.

ultrasound.title: EBUS Correlate
ultrasound.focusLabel: Right hilar target near the main bronchus entry
ultrasound.caption: If the cue feels too distal or too branch-specific, you may already be in 11R territory.

quizItems.prompt: Which station is highlighted at the right hilar checkpoint rather than the interlobar branch level?
quizItems.explanation: The correct answer is 10R because the target sits at the right hilar main-bronchus checkpoint rather than the distal interlobar branch points.

landmarkChecklist: Place 10L below 4L and before 11L.
Use the superior left main pulmonary artery margin as the upper boundary.
Make sure the target feels hilar rather than paratracheal.
This is a main-bronchus left hilar cue, not yet the interlobar branch-level target.

ct.title: Axial CT Correlate
ct.caption: Keep the target distal to the paratracheal stack but proximal to the left interlobar branch point.

bronchoscopy.title: Bronchoscopic Correlate
bronchoscopy.orientation: Left main bronchus before more distal branching.
bronchoscopy.caption: This is the gateway between the 4L paratracheal logic and 11L branch-level logic.

ultrasound.title: EBUS Correlate
ultrasound.focusLabel: Left hilar target near the main bronchus entry
ultrasound.caption: Use proximity to the main bronchus and the pulmonary artery boundary to separate 10L from 4L and 11L.

quizItems.prompt: Which station is highlighted at the left hilar checkpoint on CT?
quizItems.explanation: The correct answer is 10L because the target sits below the 4L boundary but proximal to the interlobar left branch point.

landmarkChecklist: Locate the right upper lobe bronchus and bronchus intermedius.
Confirm you are beyond the 10R hilar checkpoint.
Think branch-level anatomy, not main-bronchus anatomy.
Keep 11Rs separate from 11Ri below.

ct.title: CT Correlate
ct.focusLabel: 11Rs between the RUL bronchus and bronchus intermedius
ct.caption: Lung-window branch detail helps confirm this is the superior right interlobar substation.

bronchoscopy.title: Bronchoscopic Correlate
bronchoscopy.orientation: Right upper lobe takeoff and bronchus intermedius.
bronchoscopy.caption: This is a branch-level view beyond 10R and above 11Ri.

ultrasound.title: EBUS Correlate
ultrasound.caption: If the target still feels like a main bronchus checkpoint, reconsider 10R instead of 11Rs.

quizItems.prompt: Which station is highlighted between the right upper lobe bronchus and bronchus intermedius?
quizItems.explanation: The correct answer is 11Rs because the airway landmark is the superior right interlobar branch point between the right upper lobe bronchus and bronchus intermedius.

landmarkChecklist: Find the middle and lower lobe bronchial split.
Confirm you are more distal than 10R and more inferior than 11Rs.
Use branch anatomy rather than general hilar intuition.
This is the inferior right interlobar substation.

ct.title: CT Correlate
ct.focusLabel: 11Ri between the middle and lower lobe bronchi
ct.caption: This is the more inferior right interlobar target and should not be collapsed into a generic 11R label.

bronchoscopy.title: Bronchoscopic Correlate
bronchoscopy.orientation: Right middle and lower lobe bronchial branching.
bronchoscopy.caption: The branch detail is what distinguishes 11Ri from both 10R and 11Rs.

ultrasound.title: EBUS Correlate
ultrasound.caption: Name the branch relationship first; the station label follows from the airway anatomy.

quizItems.prompt: Which station is highlighted at the inferior right interlobar branch level?
quizItems.explanation: The correct answer is 11Ri because the target is at the inferior right interlobar branch point rather than the superior interlobar or hilar checkpoint.

landmarkChecklist: Confirm you are beyond the 10L hilar checkpoint.
Look for left branch-level airway anatomy rather than a main-bronchus view.
This is a left interlobar target, not a paratracheal one.
Use 10L as the main confusion pair.

ct.title: CT Correlate
ct.caption: The clue is branch-level left airway anatomy beyond the hilar checkpoint.

bronchoscopy.title: Bronchoscopic Correlate
bronchoscopy.orientation: Left branch-level airway beyond the main bronchus.
bronchoscopy.caption: This is more distal than 10L and should feel branch-level rather than hilar.

ultrasound.title: EBUS Correlate
ultrasound.caption: A more distal, branch-level left airway relationship supports 11L rather than 10L.

quizItems.prompt: Which station is highlighted beyond the left hilar checkpoint at the branch level?
quizItems.explanation: The correct answer is 11L because the airway cue is branch-level and distal to the 10L hilar checkpoint.
